User.title: User
User.description: Represents an individual user of the DriveCast application, holding personal preferences and saved content.

id.description: Unique identifier for the User entity.

username.description: The chosen username for the user, used for display purposes.

email.description: The email address associated with the user's account.

favoriteYouTubeChannelIds.description: An array of IDs referencing YouTubeChannel entities that the user has marked as favorite. (Relationship: User N:N YouTubeChannel)

savedYouTubeVideoIds.description: An array of IDs referencing YouTubeVideo entities that the user has saved. (Relationship: User N:N YouTubeVideo)

YouTubeChannel.title: YouTubeChannel
YouTubeChannel.description: Represents a YouTube channel that users can favorite or interact with within the app.

id.description: Unique identifier for the YouTubeChannel entity. This corresponds to the official YouTube Channel ID.

title.description: The official title or name of the YouTube channel.

thumbnailUrl.description: The URL to the channel's thumbnail image.

channelUrl.description: The URL to the official YouTube page for the channel.

YouTubeVideo.title: YouTubeVideo
YouTubeVideo.description: Represents a specific YouTube video that a user has saved for later viewing.

id.description: Unique identifier for the YouTubeVideo entity. This corresponds to the official YouTube Video ID.

title.description: The title of the YouTube video.

description.description: A brief description or summary of the video content.

thumbnailUrl.description: The URL to the video's thumbnail image.

videoUrl.description: The URL to the official YouTube page for the video.

channelId.description: Reference to the YouTubeChannel entity that published this video. (Relationship: YouTubeChannel 1:N YouTubeVideo)

publishedAt.description: The date and time when the video was published on YouTube.

definition.description: Individual user profiles, storing personal data and preferences like favorite YouTube channels and saved videos. Authorization is path-based, meaning only the authenticated user matching `{userId}` can access their own document. This achieves authorization independence and supports private data segregation.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user, corresponding to Firebase Authentication UID.

definition.description: Cached YouTube channel data for display and search within the application. This collection is globally readable by all authenticated users, allowing efficient `list` operations (QAPs) without complex hierarchical authorization dependencies. Write access is restricted to administrative services.

params.name: channelId
params.description: The unique identifier of the YouTube channel.

definition.description: Cached YouTube video data. Each document includes the `channelId` field, which is a denormalized attribute used to link videos to their respective channels directly. This enables efficient queries (e.g., fetching all videos for a given channel) and ensures Authorization Independence as no `get()` calls to parent channel documents are needed for access control. Globally readable for all authenticated users.

params.name: videoId
params.description: The unique identifier of the YouTube video.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure is designed to adhere strictly to the core design principles, particularly Authorization Independence and support for QAPs (Queries Are Not Filters). 

1.  **Users (`/users/{userId}`):** This top-level collection stores individual user profiles. Each document ID (`userId`) directly corresponds to the Firebase Authentication UID (`request.auth.uid`). This provides an extremely simple and robust authorization model: a user can only read or write their own profile document. This inherently achieves **Authorization Independence** as access to a user's data is determined solely by the path, eliminating any need for `get()` calls to check parent document ownership. User-specific preferences like `favoriteYouTubeChannelIds` and `savedYouTubeVideoIds` are stored directly within the user's document, making them private and easily accessible by the owner. QAPs are supported by only allowing reads/writes to `/users/request.auth.uid`, effectively disallowing broad `list` operations on the entire `users` collection, which would be insecure.

2.  **YouTube Channels (`/youtubeChannels/{channelId}`):** This collection serves as a cache for YouTube channel metadata. These documents are intended to be globally readable by all authenticated users. **Authorization Independence** is achieved because the data itself is public-facing (after being sourced from YouTube) and does not rely on any user-specific or parent document for access control. Write access would typically be restricted to backend services or administrators. This structure fully supports **QAPs** as `list` operations can be performed efficiently (e.g., to populate a search results page or a grid of all available channels) with simple security rules allowing `read` access for all authenticated users.

3.  **YouTube Videos (`/youtubeVideos/{videoId}`):** Similar to YouTube Channels, this collection caches YouTube video metadata. Each video document includes a `channelId` field. This `channelId` is a **denormalized** piece of information, directly linking the video to its parent channel without requiring a subcollection structure or a `get()` call to the channel document during authorization. This explicit inclusion of `channelId` within the video document is crucial for **Authorization Independence** and enables efficient queries. For example, to fetch all videos from a specific channel, a query like `collection('youtubeVideos').where('channelId', '==', 'someChannelId')` can be executed directly and securely. This again fully supports **QAPs**, allowing `list` operations for all videos within a channel with simple `read` rules for authenticated users, avoiding the performance and security pitfalls of `get()` based authorization on parent collections.
